beats.activity: Wakes up, makes coffee, and does a quick home-start routine to get ready for the rainy day.
beats.reasoning: He keeps mornings structured, and the drizzle means he prefers to settle in before heading out.

beats.activity: Drives to work and gets settled inside the library before opening.
beats.reasoning: He works in Redmond; with rain starting early, he leaves promptly and avoids lingering outside.

beats.activity: Handles morning circulation work—returns, sorting holds, and shelving under the steady library routine.
beats.reasoning: A reliable start fits his self-reliant temperament, and the weather keeps foot traffic calmer.

beats.activity: Helps a couple patrons quickly find biographies and local history resources, then checks the day’s programming prep.
beats.reasoning: He values small, trust-building interactions; staying indoors matches the drizzly morning.

beats.activity: Takes a short indoor break and scans upcoming events while planning around the bridge closure later.
beats.reasoning: He obsessively tracks ferry/commute timing, and today’s SR-520 closure makes him think ahead.

beats.activity: Walks to lunch nearby and grabs a warm meal to stay dry during the long rainy stretch.
beats.reasoning: Rain from 7am–2pm encourages a quick indoor lunch close to work.

beats.activity: Finishes desk tasks and quiet shelving, then reviews the rest of the day’s holds and returns list.
beats.reasoning: He keeps momentum after lunch rather than taking a long break in wet conditions.

beats.activity: After-work shopping for home-brewing supplies, prioritizing dry goods and avoiding long outdoor time.
beats.reasoning: With SR-520 eastbound closed 9am-noon, he’s already timed his return plans; this errand stays local and indoor-friendly.

beats.activity: Heads back toward Bellevue, choosing a detour to avoid the worst of the SR-520 backup.
beats.reasoning: Traffic snarls cross-lake due to the SR-520 closure, so he budgets extra time and reroutes.

beats.activity: Gets home, drops groceries, and does a low-key reset—drying off gear and refilling his coffee.
beats.reasoning: His energy was low yesterday; the weather and commute delays justify more decompression at home.

beats.activity: Brews a small batch at home, cleaning tools as he goes to keep the process methodical.
beats.reasoning: Home brewing is his steady competition-with-himself hobby, and it’s perfect for a rainy afternoon.

beats.activity: Plans a fly-fishing trip—checking river conditions, tying a couple flies indoors, and reviewing the next schedule.
beats.reasoning: He tracks ferry schedules obsessively and uses indoor time to prep when the weather is grey.

beats.activity: Meets close friends for an intimate dinner spot rather than a big crowd, keeping conversation the focus.
beats.reasoning: Even with rain easing after 2pm, he prefers time with friends; the market area offers indoor-friendly options without forcing a huge crowd.

beats.activity: Heads back home and showers, then organizes his brew notes and fishing plan for tomorrow.
beats.reasoning: He ends the day by setting up tomorrow’s rhythm, and the drive back keeps him off the wet streets late.

beats.activity: Wind-down at home—reads for pleasure, listens to music softly, and does a light equipment check for an early morning outing.
beats.reasoning: A calm wind-down matches his mood planning and keeps the evening comfortable after a long weather-affected day.

beats.activity: Falls asleep at home for the night.
beats.reasoning: He sleeps at home overnight, with the sleep span clearly crossing midnight as required.

diary: Grey and drizzly all morning, the kind of rain that makes every streetlight look softened at the edges. I rolled out of North Bellevue, coffee going, windshield wipers already earning their keep, and got to the Seattle Public Library—Redmond Town Center before opening. The building felt cozy in that steady way libraries do, like everything’s on a timer except the people. I spent the morning doing circulation: returns, holds, shelving, the usual rhythm. Around 8:45 I helped a couple hunt down biographies and local history—nothing fancy, just seeing their faces when the right shelf finally “clicked.” The SR-520 closure hit my brain before it hit the commute; I kept checking the route and ducked out for lunch at Whole Foods to stay dry. Later, I detoured home, then brewed a small batch and tied a couple flies. Dinner with close friends at Pike Place Market was the bright spot—warm food, warm talk. I ended the day with my plans sorted and a calm, satisfied feeling.
thought_process: I leaned into the library routine early because it’s the one thing that doesn’t require me to wrestle the weather or traffic. When the SR-520 bridge closure started at 9 and ran until noon, I could feel the ripple even while I was shelving—people asking questions, me mentally preparing alternate routes. I chose indoor breaks and quick errands (Whole Foods, then Safeway for dry brewing supplies) mostly to protect my time and keep my mood steady; the rain was already draining enough. World events—at least the city’s version of them, like emergency repairs—made the day feel a little compressed, so I planned around it instead of fighting it. My mood shifted from focused to lightly restless during the midday route detour, then softened again after dinner and the quiet ritual of brewing and fly-tying.
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
